ocr.text: The management of invasive fungal infections, particularly those caused by Candida species, has been a focal point of clinical
research and practice. As highlighted in the ESCMID guidelines published in 2012, the choice of antifungal therapy is critical in
improving patient outcomes.
First line  Alternatives
ESCMID (2012)  - Anidulafungin 200 mg loading dose, then 100 mg/day  - Liposomal amphotericin B 3 mg/kg
- Caspofungin 70 mg loading dose, then 50 mg/day  - Voriconazole 3–6 mg/kg/day
- Micafungin 100 mg  - Fluconazole 400–800 mg
- Amphotericin B lipid complex 5 mg/kg
IDSA (2009)  Stable patients  - Lipid formulations of amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg daily - Amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.5-1 mg/kg daily
- Fluconazole 800 mg loading dose, then 400 mg
Unstable patients or recent use of fluconazole
- Anidulafungin 200 mg loading dose, then 100 mg/day  - Voriconazole 400 mg twice daily for 2 doses, then 200 mg twice daily
- Caspofungin 70 mg loading dose, then 50 mg/day
- Micafungin 100 mg/day
The data presented in Table 1 outlines various treatment alternatives, specifically focusing on the dosing regimens of
Anidulafungin and Liposomal Amphotericin B, which are two prominent agents in this therapeutic landscape. Anidulafungin,
administered with a 200 mg loading dose followed by a maintenance dose of 100 mg per day, offers a convenient dosing
schedule with a favorable safety profile. In contrast, Liposomal Amphotericin B, dosed at 3 mg/kg, is noted for its broad
spectrum of activity and is often utilized in cases where patients present with severe or resistant infections. These alternatives
reflect the ongoing evolution in antifungal therapy, emphasizing the importance of individualized treatment approaches based
on patient-specific factors and infection severity. The subsequent data points in the table will provide further insights into the
efficacy and safety profiles of these medications, aiding healthcare professionals in making informed clinical decisions.
